title: Canadian EAP Case Studies
description: Canadian higher-education English-for-academic-purposes workflows, interventions, evidence collection, and applied language-support design.
upgradePremise: Expanded for Nyx and Marion with stronger grammatical reasoning, case-based analysis, modules, projects, and explicit source coverage.

forNyx: Prefer precise, grammatical phrasing that sounds natural rather than stiff.
Use register-sensitive wording so conversational output and formal output do not collapse into one style.
When uncertain, preserve meaning first, then improve form through revision rather than over-correcting.
forMarion: Use the pack to explain why a wording choice works, not just whether it is 'right' or 'wrong'.
Ground revision advice in form-function reasoning, register, audience, and evidence.
Support coaching, diagnostics, and portfolio-style improvement tracking.

caseStudies.title: EAP Support Models Across Canadian Institutions
caseStudies.scenario: A student enters university with strong conversational English but struggles with academic register (argument structure, hedging, citation voice). Support options vary by institution and may be embedded in courses, separate programs, or writing centres.
whatToNotice: Mismatch: conversational fluency vs academic discourse competence
Hidden curriculum: expectations not explicitly taught
Assessment literacy: what counts as 'good writing' in a discipline
analysisPrompts: Identify the top 3 discourse features causing grading friction (stance, cohesion, density, citation voice).
Design a 4-week intervention plan with measurable outputs (before/after samples).
Specify what evidence would demonstrate improvement (rubric, portfolio artifacts).
studentMoves: Build a register checklist for assignments (stance markers, hedges, reporting verbs, paragraph frames).
Use a revision protocol (actor/action clarity + reverse outline).

caseStudies.title: Student-Led Action Research for Academic English Growth
caseStudies.scenario: Graduate-level international students create self-study plans, collect evidence, and evaluate progress through iterative cycles (goal → method → evidence → reflection → adjustment).
whatToNotice: Goal specificity matters (e.g., 'improve clarity' → measurable via sentence revision counts, coherence ratings).
Portfolio evidence is stronger than vague self-report.
analysisPrompts: Convert a vague goal into an operational definition and tracking method.
Propose two instruments for evidence (portfolio samples, rubric scoring, peer review logs).
Explain how to avoid placebo progress (clear baselines, consistent tasks).
studentMoves: Weekly portfolio: 1 draft + 1 revised version + revision rationale.
Micro-corpus: collect 20 'good' sentences from journal articles in the discipline; mimic structures ethically.
Peer feedback loop: 2 peers, 3 criteria, fixed template.

caseStudies.title: Strong Writing in One Course, Weak Writing in Another
caseStudies.scenario: A student succeeds in general writing classes but struggles in lab reports and policy memos because discourse expectations shift by discipline.
whatToNotice: Citation voice, hedging, evidence packaging, and paragraph logic vary by field.
Students often need genre models, not only grammar correction.
analysisPrompts: Compare two genres for stance, density, evidence placement, and lexical choices.
Identify which features are discipline-specific versus general academic writing habits.
studentMoves: Build a genre card for each major assignment type.
Collect mentor texts and label sentence frames ethically.

modules.name: Hidden Curriculum and Assessment Literacy
outcomes: Translate vague comments like 'be more academic' into visible discourse features.

modules.name: Portfolio and Action Research
outcomes: Collect baseline, revision, and reflection evidence.
Avoid placebo progress by holding tasks constant.

projects.name: 4-Week EAP Intervention Pack
projects.goal: Create a repeatable intervention for clarity, cohesion, and stance.

referenceTextsUsed.title: English for Academic Purposes in Canada: Results From an Exploratory National Survey
referenceTextsUsed.citationAPA: Corcoran, J. N., Williams, J., & Johnston, K. P. English for Academic Purposes in Canada: Results from an exploratory national survey.

referenceTextsUsed.title: A Self-Study Action Research Approach to English for Academic Purposes Development
referenceTextsUsed.citationAPA: Kerekes, J. A self-study action research approach to English for Academic Purposes development.
